serving.natural_language_portion: One small bowl of spicy pork intestine typically weighs approximately 150 g and constitutes a single side-dish or main serving at a hawker stall or Chinese restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo Database — Spicy Pork Intestine (Small Bowl), cr_id G00010513
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Spicy Pork Intestine (Small Bowl), cr_id G00010513, with recorded confidence 0.95. HPB is an approved primary regional source for Singapore dishes. Saturated fat (3.5 g/100 g) is not reported by HPB for this entry; it is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for pork large intestine (FDC ID 168370), which is an approved secondary global source, and is documented as an approximation in notes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index not applicable — negligible carbohydrate content confirmed by HPB Singapore Foodo Database (cr_id G00010513)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Spicy Pork Intestine (Small Bowl) contains 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g per HPB Foodo database (cr_id G00010513). Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates; no GI testing is meaningful for a zero-carbohydrate food. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by definition. Per the University of Sydney GI Database methodology, foods with negligible available carbohydrate are classified as GI not applicable. glycemic_index set to null; gi_category set to not_applicable.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg per 100 g — far exceeding the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; a single small bowl (150 g) delivers approximately 1575 mg sodium, representing over 68% of the 2300 mg daily limit, and the minimal potassium content (47 mg/100 g) provides no meaningful counter-regulatory benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and sugar content is glycaemically neutral, but high cholesterol and saturated fat elevate metabolic risk
type2_diabetes.guidance: The dish contains 0 g carbohydrate and 0 g sugar per 100 g, posing no direct glycaemic spike; however, the cholesterol content (89 mg/100 g) and estimated saturated fat (~3.5 g/100 g) are concerns for the insulin-resistant metabolic profile, and the very high sodium (1050 mg/100 g) worsens cardiovascular co-morbidity risk in type 2 diabetes patients.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (1050 mg/100 g), dietary cholesterol (89 mg/100 g), and saturated fat from pork organ meat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid this dish or consume only very small portions infrequently; the combination of extreme sodium load, dietary cholesterol from pork intestine, and saturated fat contributes to elevated blood pressure, LDL cholesterol, and arterial inflammation risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory organ meat (arachidonic acid, saturated fat), nightshade-derived chili and doubanjiang, and high sodium worsening tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Pork intestine is a red-meat organ rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6) that amplifies inflammatory cytokine production; combined with chili and doubanjiang (nightshade-derived), which may trigger joint sensitivity, and sodium at 1050 mg/100 g worsening RA-related tissue swelling, this dish is not recommended for rheumatoid arthritis patients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spicy Pork Intestine (Small Bowl), known in Chinese as 小碗肥肠 (xiǎo wǎn féi cháng), is a Chinese braised and stir-fried pork offal dish popular in Singapore hawker centres and Chinese restaurants. The dish features cleaned pork large intestine that is slow-braised or stir-fried with doubanjiang (spicy fermented bean paste), dried chili, soy sauce, garlic, ginger, and Sichuan peppercorn, producing a deeply savoury, spicy, and slightly numbing flavour profile with a tender yet chewy texture. Per 100 g, the dish provides 171 kcal, 19.1 g protein, 10.5 g fat, 0 g carbohydrate, 0 g sugar, 0 g dietary fibre, 89 mg cholesterol, and 1050 mg sodium. The zero carbohydrate content means the dish has no glycaemic impact, but the extremely high sodium content (1050 mg/100 g) makes it unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The high cholesterol and saturated fat from pork organ meat are additional concerns for metabolic and cardiovascular health. The dish is not halal. It is typically served as a main dish or side dish at lunch or dinner. Clinically, the sodium content is the primary concern, with a single small bowl (approximately 150 g) delivering approximately 1575 mg sodium, exceeding two-thirds of the recommended daily sodium limit.
